question.text: What is the difference between Part A and Part B of the Schedule of offences under PMLA?

answer.text: Part A offences trigger money laundering provisions regardless of the amount involved, while Part B offences require the total value involved to be one crore rupees or more
Part A covers offences committed by individuals, while Part B covers offences committed by companies
Part A covers domestic offences, while Part B covers only cross-border offences
Part A covers financial crimes, while Part B covers violent crimes only

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 2(1)(y) of PMLA, Part A scheduled offences attract money laundering provisions without any monetary threshold. Part B offences (such as certain customs violations) require the total value involved to be one crore rupees or more to qualify as scheduled offences under PMLA. **The Trap:** Option C is tempting because Part C does deal with cross-border implications, but the actual distinction between Parts A and B is about monetary thresholds, not geographical scope. **Cross-Exam Context:** The three-part structure of the Schedule (A, B, C) and their thresholds are commonly tested in NISM IFSCA-01. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When assessing transaction risks, remember that high-value transactions may trigger PMLA even for Part B offences — the one crore threshold is cumulative for the total value involved in the offence.
